description: Homalomena ‘Selby’ is a tropical ornamental houseplant in the Araceae family, making it toxic to cats for the same reason as Dieffenbachia, Philodendron, and Pothos: insoluble calcium oxalate crystals (raphides) throughout the leaves and stems. When chewed, these needle-like crystals are forcefully ejected into the oral mucosa, causing intense immediate irritation.
name: Homalomena Selby / Homalomena rubescens
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: insoluble calcium oxalate raphides; the plant’s cell sap also contains proteolytic enzymes
Mechanism: crystal penetration of the mouth and throat lining causes mechanical burning and inflammation
Symptoms: sudden oral pain, intense drooling, pawing at mouth, swollen lips or tongue, difficulty swallowing, vomiting
All parts of the plant are hazardous; the reaction is immediate and self-limiting
Rinse the mouth with water; seek veterinary care if swallowing is impaired or symptoms do not resolve
